2018-10-29 pathology_report **PATHOLOGY REPORT** --- ### Patient Information **Name:** Sara O’Connor **Age/Sex:** 70‑yr Female ### Specimen Identification **Accession #:** NP‑001‑202X‑01 **Specimen Type:** Endoscopic core‐needle biopsies of nasopharyngeal mass (right posterior wall) **Site of Collection:** Nasopharynx – right lateral wall adjacent to eustachian tube opening **Clinical History:** Newly identified nasopharyngeal lesion causing profuse epistaxis; imaging suggests locally advanced disease (T4 N2 M0). Total aggregate dimensions 2.3 cm × 1.5 cm × 0.6 cm; cumulative weight ≈ 0.35 g. **Nasopharyngeal carcinoma**, non‑keratinising *undifferentiated* type (World Health Organization Grade III), **EBV‑associated** (diffusely EBER‑positive). - Histologic features correspond to classic “lymphoepithelioma.” - High proliferative activity (Ki‑67≈80%) and expression of basal/squamous markers (p63, CK5/6)
2019-04-03 clinical_note --- #### History of Present Illness Sara O’Connor is a 70‑year‑old woman who was diagnosed **two weeks ago** with World Health Organization (WHO) type III nasopharyngeal carcinoma (non‑keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma, undifferentiated), EBV‑EBER positive, staged cT4 N2 M0 (Stage IV). She has not yet initiated definitive therapy. Associated symptoms include mild frontal headache, lightheadedness, and nausea without vomiting. Given known invasive nasopharyngeal malignancy, the working impression is **tumor‑associated hemorrhage** with ongoing risk of rebleeding. **Massive epistaxis secondary to locally advanced nasopharyngeal carcinoma (cT4 N2 M0, EBV‑positive).** Acute blood loss anemia partially corrected with PRBCs. Hemostasis currently achieved via combined anterior/posterior nasal packing. High likelihood of re‑bleed given tumor friability and skull‑base involvement. **Nasopharyngeal carcinoma – newly diagnosed, untreated.** Molecular profile (NGS available): TP53 R273H, PIK3CA H1047R, CDKN2A loss, EGFR amp., FGFR1 amp., NOTCH2 truncating mutation; KRAS WT. EBV DNA elevated. **Hypertension**, well‑controlled on lisinopril. **Anemia, acute** – responding to transfusion
2019-04-04 clinical_note --- #### History of Present Illness Sara is a 70‑year‑old woman who presented earlier today with continued heavy nosebleeds originating from her right nostril. She initially sought emergency care approximately six hours ago when brisk epistaxis resulted in an estimated blood loss of ≈600 mL, prompting placement of anterior/posterior nasal packs and transfusion of 2 units PRBCs. She has been diagnosed this week with World Health Organization (WHO) Type III non‑keratinizing undifferentiated nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC), staged clinically as T4 N2 M0 (Stage IV). The diagnosis was confirmed via core needle biopsy demonstrating sheets of pleomorphic epithelioid cells with prominent nucleoli, abundant lymphoid infiltrates, and strong diffuse EBER in situ hybridisation confirming Epstein–Barr virus association. Conservative measures (additional packing, topical vasoconstrictors) have failed to achieve durable control. Our multidisciplinary discussion (Radiology, Interventional Radiology, ENT surgery) concluded that prompt super‑selective arterial embolization offers the best chance to arrest bleeding while preserving surrounding structures ahead of planned curative intent chemoradiation. Prominent opacification of the ipsilateral sphenopalatine foramen suggestive of vascular involvement. These images support tumor infiltration of the sphenopalatine artery territory as probable culprit for refractory bleeding. **Nasopharyngeal carcinoma, WHO type III, T4 N2 M0 (stage IV)** with active tumor‑related epistaxis refractory to conventional packing. Acute symptomatic anemia secondary to ongoing blood loss (Hb 9.6 g/dL). Controlled essential hypertension. **Future Oncologic Management** - After hemostasis achieved, anticipate initiation of definitive intensity‑modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) combined with concurrent weekly cisplatin (40 mg/m²) per institutional protocol
2019-08-22 clinical_note **Oncology Progress Note – Follow‑Up Visit** **Patient:** Sara O’Connor **DOB:** [redacted] **MRN:** [redacted] **Date of Service:** [will be inserted automatically] --- ### Chief Complaint “Bleeding from my nose has gotten less severe, but I’m still having occasional spots of blood.” --- ### History of Present Illness Sara is a 71‑year‑old woman who was diagnosed last year with WHO Type III (non‑keratinizing undifferentiated) nasopharyngeal carcinoma, cT4 N2 M0, EBV‑EBER positive. She subsequently underwent: * Super‑selective embolization of the right sphenopalatine artery (age 71). * Induction chemotherapy with cisplatin 100 mg/m² d1 + 5‑FU 1000 mg/m²/day continuous infusion d1–d4 (PF) q21 days. After Cycle 1 she developed grade 3 thrombocytopenia (platelet nadir 45 K/µL); doses were reduced to cisplatin 80 mg/m² and 5‑FU 800 mg/m²/day thereafter. She presents today for her scheduled “post‑cycle” evaluation following Cycle 2 of the modified PF regimen. The patient reports that a repeat nasal endoscopy performed yesterday demonstrated *decreased tumor bulk* compared with baseline, yet she continues to experience **intermittent epistaxis**, typically limited to a few drops per day without associated dizziness or syncope. EBV viral load halved, correlating with observed decrease in tumor volume. - Invasion into clivus/skull base persists but no progression of bony erosion. - No new intracranial extension or dural involvement. Overall impression: **Partial radiographic response** to induction PF; stable osseous infiltration. **Nasopharyngeal Carcinoma – WHO Type III, currently responding to induction PF** - Decrease in endoscopic tumor bulk and plasma EBV DNA suggests biologic activity suppression. - Persistent intermittent epistaxis most likely secondary to residual friable neovasculature rather than coagulopathy (platelets adequate). **Chemotherapy‑induced thrombocytopenia – Resolved** - Platelet count recovered to >100 K after dose modification; no current requirement for growth factor support. **Hypertension – Controlled** - Blood pressure stable on current ACE inhibitor; no cisplatin‑related nephrotoxicity detected. **Iron‑deficiency anemia – Stable** - Hemoglobin unchanged; compliance with oral iron confirmed. Oncology Treatment** - Proceed with **Cycle 3 of modified PF** (cisplatin 80 mg/m² d1, 5‑FU 800 mg/m²/day CI d1–d4). - Re‑image with MRI after Cycle 4 (mid‑point) to confirm trend toward further shrinkage before transitioning to definitive intensity‑modulated radiotherapy (planned total dose 66 Gy)
2019-10-19 imaging_report **Patient:** Sara O’Connor **Age/Sex:** 70/F **MRN:** ********* --- ### Imaging Modality & Technique Contrast‑enhanced magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the nasopharynx was performed on a 1.5 Tesla system. --- ### Clinical Indication Evaluation of residual nasopharyngeal tumor burden and assessment of ongoing epistaxis risk following recent super‑selective arterial embolization and induction chemotherapy (cisplatin/5‑FU). Maximal axial dimension measured **2.8 cm** (AP × transverse × craniocaudal ≈ 2.8 × 2.2 × 2.6 cm). - Infiltration into the **skull base** is evident, with cortical breach of the clival bone and mild erosions of the adjoining sphenoidal rostrum. #### Cervical Lymph Nodes - Bilateral level II–III cervical chains demonstrate reactive‑appearing lymph nodes up to 1.0 cm short axis; none display central necrosis or abnormal enhancement. **Residual nasopharyngeinal carcinoma** – 2.8‑cm enhancing mass involving the right fossa of Rosenmüller with direct skull‑base invasion. Appearance unchanged relative to prior examination, indicating *stable disease*. Persistent proximity to critical neurovascular structures confers ongoing risk for further epistaxis despite previous embolization. No new locoregional nodal enlargement or distant intracranial/metastatic lesions identified
2020-02-13 ngs_report **Next‑Generation Sequencing (NGS) Report – Comprehensive Genomic Profiling** --- ### Patient Information - **Name:** Sara O’Connor - **Age/Sex:** 70‑yr / Female ### Specimen Details | Item | Description | |--------------------------|-------------| | **Specimen Identifier** | NPX‑001 (Nasopharyngeal Core Biopsy) | | **Source** | FFPE block from initial diagnostic nasopharyngeal biopsy (undifferentiated non‑keratinizing carcinoma, WHO type III) | | **Tumor Content** | Estimated ≥60 % neoplastic cellularity (based on H&E slide review) | | **Extraction Method** | Automated nucleic acid extraction (Qiagen AllPrep DNA/RNA FFPE Kit) | ### Assay Overview - **Platform:** Hybrid capture–based targeted NGS panel covering >300 cancer‐relevant genes (including full coding regions, selected introns, and select promoter regions). ### Detected Somatic Alterations | Gene | Variant (HGVS) | Protein Change | Allele Frequency* | Classification | |------|---------------|----------------|-------------------|----------------| | **TP53** | c.818 G>A | p.Arg273His | 38 % | Likely pathogenic (hotspot missense, dominant negative) | | **PIK3CA** | c.3140 A>G | p.His1047Arg | 27 % | Likely pathogenic (canonical activating hotspot) | | **CDKN2A** | Homozygous deletion | — | — | Pathogenic (loss of tumor suppressor) | | **EGFR** | Copy-number gain | Amplified (≈ 12 copies) | — | Potentially actionable (amplification) | | **FGFR1** | Copy-number gain | Amplified (≈ 9 copies) | — | Potentially actionable | | **NOTCH2** | c.634 C>T | p.Arg212Ter | 22 % | Likely pathogenic (nonsense truncation) | | **KRAS** | Wild‑type | — | — | — | | **EBV DNA Load (quantitative PCR)** | 2.3 × 10⁴ copies/mL plasma | — | — | Consistent with EBV‑positive nasopharyngeal carcinoma | \*Variant allele frequency calculated relative to total sequenced alleles at the locus. **TP53 p.R273H** – This missense alteration occurs at a well‑characterized DNA‑binding domain hotspot and confers loss of normal p53 function while exerting dominant‑negative effects. It is commonly observed in head‑and‑neck squamous malignancies and correlates with aggressive behavior. **PIK3CA p.H1047R** – An established activating mutation driving constitutive PI3K‑AKT signaling. Frequently identified in epithelial tumors including nasopharyngeal carcinoma; may sensitize tumors to PI3K inhibitors under investigational protocols. **CDKN2A homozygous loss** – Results in complete abrogation of p16^INK4a^ and p14^ARF^ expression, facilitating unchecked cyclin‑D/CDK4/6 activity and evasion of senescence/apoptosis pathways. **EGFR amplification** – High-level focal increase in EGFR copy number has been documented in subsets of EBV‑associated nasopharyngeal carcinomas. While anti‑EGFR monoclonal antibodies show modest efficacy in unselected head‑and‑neck SCC, the predictive value of isolated amplification remains uncertain. **FGFR1 amplification** – May contribute to MAPK/ERK pathway activation. Targeted FGFR inhibition is being explored clinically; however, current FDA‑approved agents lack indication for nasopharyngeal carcinoma. **NOTCH2 truncating mutation (p.R212*)** – Predicted loss‑of‑function; Notch signaling plays complex roles in differentiation of nasopharyngeal epithelium. Loss may cooperate with other oncogenic drivers. **EBV DNA quantitative level** – Plasma EBV DNA serves as a surrogate marker of tumor burden in endemic nasopharyngeal carcinoma. The measured load aligns with known baseline levels for stage IV disease. Overall, the molecular profile demonstrates multiple co‑existing oncogenic lesions affecting cell‑cycle regulation (CDKN2A loss, TP53 mutation), proliferative signaling (PIK3CA, EGFR, FGFR1 amplifications), and developmental pathways (NOTCH2 truncation). Such complexity underscores the biologically heterogeneous nature of advanced nasopharyngeal carcinoma. *Plasma Epstein-Barr virus DNA as a biomarker for nasopharyngeal carcinoma.* J Clin Oncol
2020-07-01 clinical_note --- ### History of Present Illness Sara is a 72‑year‑old woman with newly diagnosed, EBV‑positive, WHO Type III nasopharyngeal carcinoma (T4 N2 M0, Stage IV). She underwent super‑selective sphenopalatine artery embolization followed by induction PF chemotherapy beginning at age 71. After the first cycle she experienced Grade 3 thrombocytopenia (platelet nadir 48 K/µL) leading to a 25% dose reduction of both agents; subsequent counts stabilized above 110 K/µL. She has now completed three cycles of the modified PF regimen (cisplatin 75 mg/m² Day 1, 5‑FU 800 mg/m²/day Continuous Infusion Days 1–4 q21d). Endoscopic evaluation performed last week demonstrates a partially regressed nasopharyngeal mass but continued oozing from ulcerated areas overlying the posterior choanae. Given refractory hemorrhage and incomplete local control, definitive intensity‑modulated radiotherapy (IMRT) is being considered. - Invasion through the sphenoid sinus floor into the skull base persists but no overt intracranial extension. - Post‑contrast signal intensity modestly decreased compared with pretreatment study, suggesting partial response. Interpretation: Stable disease per RECIST criteria, however clinically significant bleeding continues. **Nasopharyngeal carcinoma, WHO type III, EBV‑positive – locally advanced (Stage IV)** - Completed 3 cycles of modified PF; partial anatomical response but persistent symptomatic epistaxis. **Anemia secondary to chronic nasal blood loss** (Hb 10.2 g/dL) 4. **EBV viral load markedly declining**, indicating biologic activity suppression. Overall disease burden is controlled anatomically yet the bleeding risk mandates definitive locoregional therapy. **Definitive Radiotherapy** - Proceed with IMRT targeting the nasopharyngeal primary and involved nodes to total dose **66 Gy in 33 fractions**. - Initiate simulation CT next week; contouring to include spared critical structures (optic chiasm, brainstem). - Concurrent weekly cisplatin **40 mg/m²** starting Week 1 of radiation, contingent on adequate marrow reserve (Platelets ≥100 K/µL, Creatinine ≤1.5 mg/dL)
2020-09-22 clinical_note **Oncology Progress Note – Follow‑up Visit** --- ### Patient Sara O’Connor Female, 73 y/o --- #### **Chief Complaint** Evaluation of post‑treatment PET‑CT performed 2 weeks ago and discussion of newly identified left parotid region uptake. O’Connor returns for routine surveillance following completion of definitive intensity‑modulated radiotherapy (IMRT) to 66 Gy delivered over 33 fractions with concurrent weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m² (completed 1 month ago). She tolerated the course overall, although she required temporary enteral feeds for Grade 4 oral mucositis lasting approximately 14 days; her diet has returned to solid foods without dysphagia. - Left parotid region mildly tender to palpation; a firm, mobile nodule ≈1 cm palpable just inferior to the mandibular angle, non‑fluctuant, no overlying skin changes. **Oral Cavity:** Mild residual mucosal erythema along buccal mucosa, healing well; dentition adequate. **Findings:** - **Nasopharynx / Primary Site:** Markedly reduced metabolic activity compared with baseline. Residual thickening of the nasopharyngeal wall demonstrates faint peripheral uptake (SUV_max ≈ 2.1) compatible with post‑therapeutic inflammatory change rather than viable tumour. No evidence of osseous erosion or intracranial extension. - **Left Parotid Region:** A solitary, intensely FDG‑avid lymph node measuring 1.2 cm × 0.9 cm located within level Ia of the left parotid chain. SUV_max ≈ 6.5, higher than background salivary tissue. No adjacent extranodal spread visualized. - **Distant Sites:** No hypermetabolic foci in thorax, abdomen, pelvis, bone marrow, or brain. ** impression:** Near‑complete metabolic remission of the nasopharyngeal primary. Isolated FDG‑avid left Level Ia node representing probable early regional metastatic involvement (cN1). No overt distant disease detected. **Recurrent/metastatic nasopharyngeal carcinoma – Regional nodal relapse (left parotid/Level Ia).** - Prior definitive chemoradiation achieved excellent control of the primary. The current isolated nodal disease constitutes a locoregional failure. Molecular profile remains unchanged (TP53 R273H, PIK3CA H1047R, CDKN2A loss, EGFR/FGR1 amplifications); EBV DNA continues to decline, suggesting modest tumour burden. **Post‑radiation sequelae – Resolved Grade 4 mucositis**, now clinically quiescent. **Surgical Salvage** – Consider selective left supraomohyoid neck dissection targeting Level Ia node. Pre‑operative work‑up to include fine‑needle aspiration (FNA) of the suspicious node for confirmation and HPV/EBV ISH. **Systemic Therapy** – Initiate second‑line platinum doublet while awaiting surgical decision: Gemcitabine 1000 mg/m² d1 & d8 + Cisplatin 75 mg/m² d1 q21 days (as per institutional protocol). **Targeted/Immune Approach** – Given EGFR amplification and high PD‑L1 expression documented on prior archival sample (≥50%), discuss eligibility for off‑label cetuximab ± pembrolizumab trial; however, prior systemic exposure may limit enrollment
2021-02-24 clinical_note **Oncology Progress Note** **Patient:** Sara O’Connor **MRN:** ██████ **DOB/Age:** 70 yr / Current Age: 74 yr **Date of Service:** [to be inserted] --- ### Chief Complaint Follow‑up appointment after completion of two cycles of gemcitabine + cisplatin; review of interim CT neck/chest performed 3 weeks ago. --- ### History of Present Illness Sara is a 74‑year‑old woman with a known diagnosis of WHO Type III non‑keratinizing undifferentiated nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC) originally staged cT4 N2 M0 (Stage IV) at age 70. She has undergone: * Endoscopic debulking and super‑selective sphenopalatine artery embolization (age 71) * Induction PF (cisplatin + 5‑FU) → stopped early after grade 3 thrombocytopenia (dose reduced) * Definitive intensity‑modulated RT 66 Gy/33fx with concurrent weekly cisplatin (age 72) complicated by grade 4 mucositis * Post‑RT PET‑CT demonstrating near complete metabolic response of the primary but emergence of a 1.2 cm FDJ‑avid left parotid node (new regional recurrence) (age 73) * Second‑line gemcitabine 1000 mg/m² d1/d8 + cisplatin 75 mg/m² d1 q21d initiated at age 73; tolerated well except a single episode of grade 3 cisplatin‑associated hypertension managed with temporary nifedipine drip and subsequent oral lisinopril titration. She presents today asymptomatic aside from mild fatigue. She continues her home antihypertensive regimen (lisinopril 20 mg daily) without recent dosage adjustment. Interim cross‑sectional imaging obtained 3 weeks ago (contrast‑enhanced CT neck and chest) demonstrates: * Parotid region: Left Level II–III lymph node now measuring 0.9 cm × 0.7 cm, up from 1.2 cm pre‑treatment—a ≥30% decrease in longest diameter meeting RECIST criteria for *partial response*. * Pulmonary fields: New subcentimeter solid nodules bilaterally — right upper lobe posterior segment (0.6 cm), left lower lobe basal segment (0.5 cm), and left middle lobe anterior segment (0.4 cm). Prior scans did not demonstrate these foci. Overall disease burden reflects a **mixed response**: favorable regression of the known regional node while emerging pulmonary micronodules raise concern for early hematogenous spread. Left Level II–III node size ↓30%, central necrosis absent. No evidence of carotid encasement or intracranial extension. Radiology impression: Partial response of treated parotid nodal disease; appearance of new bilateral pulmonary micronodules most compatible with early metastatic deposits versus inflammatory granulomas. Correlation with clinical context favors metastases. **Recurrent NPC – Regional Node** – Demonstrating partial response to gemcitabine + cisplatin (RECIST PR). Good tolerability apart from controlled hypertension. **New Bilateral Pulmonary Micronodules** – Likely early distant metastases (stage IVB); however volume limited and indeterminate nature warrants close surveillance. **Hypertension, Controlled** – Previously exacerbated during cisplatin infusions; presently stable on lisinopril 20 mg PO QD. **Labs:** Adequate marrow reserve and renal function for continued platinum doublet; slight rise in EBV DNA suggests incremental viral shedding but below thresholds predictive of rapid progression
2021-05-24 imaging_report **Chest Computed Tomography – High‑Resolution Protocol** --- ### Patient Female, 70‑year‑old (age at time of examination: 75) ### Referring Physician / Ordering Service Medical Oncology ### Clinical History / Indication Surveillance of known pulmonary metastatic deposits secondary to nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Prior high‑resolution chest CT performed 6 months ago documented three subcentimeter solid nodules. * **Radiation Dose:** Volume CT dose index (CTDIvol) ≈ 3.2 mGy; DLP ≈ 120 mGy·cm. * **Comparison:** Non‑contrast thin‑section HRCT dated six months prior. No evidence of interval growth when compared with prior study; measurements unchanged within ≤0.1 cm. No additional pulmonary nodules detected throughout remaining lobes. No enlarged hilar, mediastinal, or supraclavicular lymph nodes (short axis >1 cm). No osteoblastic or lytic lesions identified. Stable appearance of three previously characterized subcentimeter solid pulmonary nodules (largest 0.9 cm); no measurable progression over 6‑month interval. Imaging features remain indeterminate but compatible with limited metastatic involvement versus benign granulomatous foci. No new intrathoracic disease—mediastinal and hilar stations free of pathologic adenopathy; pleura intact. These findings support continued surveillance of the pulmonary lesions per existing oncologic plan.
2021-10-24 clinical_note **Oncology Progress Note – Follow‑up Visit** --- ### Patient Sara O’Connor Female Age 75 ### Date of Service [Date omitted per protocol] --- ## CHIEF COMPLAINT Evaluation of disease status and discussion of therapeutic options, including eligibility for investigational clinical trials. O’Connor is a 75‑year‑old woman with a longstanding diagnosis of WHO Type III non‑keratinizing undifferentiated nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC) initially staged cT4 N2 M0 (Stage IV). Over the past five years she has undergone multimodality therapy: * **Induction PF (cisplatin + 5‑FU)** with subsequent super‑selective sphenopalatine artery embolization for severe epistaxis. * **Definitive intensity‑modulated radiotherapy (IMRT) 66 Gy/33 fx** with concurrent weekly cisplatin. * **Second‑line gemcitabine/cisplatin**, complicated by transient hypertension and development of bilateral subcentimeter pulmonary nodules. Most recently, a high‑resolution chest CT performed at age 75 identified three peripheral pulmonary nodules ≤ 0.9 cm without measurable growth compared with prior imaging (stable disease). A PET–CT obtained after completion of chemoradiation demonstrated excellent metabolic response of the primary site but revealed a new FDG‑avid 1.2‑cm left parotid lymph node, subsequently treated with gemcitabine/cisplatin resulting in partial regression of that node. She continues to experience occasional minor epistaxis despite prior embolization, prompting concern for future hemorrhagic complications. There is no evidence of progressive locoregional disease on recent nasopharyngoscopic examination. Given her cumulative exposure to platinum‑based regimens and the presence of residual metastatic lesions (pulmonary nodules), Ms. O’Conner was evaluated today for potential enrolment in phase II/III immuno‑oncology trials targeting recurrent/metastatic NPC. O’Connor has already received both frontline PF and salvage gemcitabine/platinum following progression, she does not meet eligibility for those particular trials. ## IMAGING SUMMARY - **High‑Resolution Chest CT (performed 2 mo ago):** Three bilateral subpleural nodules (largest 0.9 cm); unchanged size/composition versus prior study → “Stable”. - **Recent Nasopharyngeal Endoscopy (within last month):** Minimal residual friable mucosa at former tumor bed; no overt mass effect. - **PET‑CT (post‑IMRT, 6 mo prior):** Markedly diminished SUV uptake at primary site (SUV_max 2.1 vs pre‑treatment 13.5). Persistent mildly avid left parotid node resolved partially after gemcitabine/cisplatin (ΔSUV 45%). No new hypermetabolic foci elsewhere. Overall impression: Locoregional disease under reasonable control; distant pulmonary micrometastases remain indolent. **Recurrent/Metastatic Non‑Keratinizing Undifferentiated NPC** – currently stable disease with low-volume pulmonary involvement. Prior systemic exposures (PF induction, gemcitabine/cisplatin) render her **ineligible for most contemporary early‑phase trials mandating naïve recurrent disease**. **Pulmonary Nodules** – presumed oligometastatic NPC deposits, stable on serial CTs. **Supportive Care Needs** – Mild chronic fatigue and xerostomia secondary to prior irradiation. **Medication Review** - **Antihypertensives:** Lisinopril 10 mg daily (BP stable). **Patient Education & Goals of Care** Reviewed prognosis associated with stage IV NPC after multiple recurrences
2022-01-11 clinical_note --- ### History of Present Illness Sara is a 76‑year‑old woman with WHO Type III non‑keratinizing undifferentiated nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC) originally staged cT4 N2 M0 (Stage IV) diagnosed at age 70. Over the past six years she has undergone: * Super‑selective sphenopalatine artery embolization + PF induction chemo (cisplatin/5‑FU) → Grade 3 thrombocytopenia, dose reductions. * Definitive intensity‑modulated RT 66 Gy with concurrent weekly cisplatin → Grade 4 mucositis, required temporary PEG feed. * Second‑line gemcitabine/cisplatin → Partial response of left parotid node, emergence of bilateral subcentimeter pulmonary nodules (stable on serial scans). * Multiple hospitalizations for acute epistaxis, most recently managed conservatively with nasal packing and blood product support. Since her last clinic encounter (age 75) she has been maintained on low‑molecular‑weight heparin (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC daily) for a provoked left femoral DVT (grade 2) successfully anticoagulated without recurrence. No additional systemic anticancer agents have been administered owing to cumulative toxicity and lack of trial eligibility. Impression: indeterminate metastatic pulmonary lesions, currently stable. - **MRI Head/Neck (performed 8 weeks ago):** Residual enhancement of nasopharyngeal wall measuring 2.5 cm, abutting skull base without frank intracranial extension. No new cranial nerve involvement identified. **Recurrent/Metastatic Non‑Keratinizing Undifferentiated Nasopharyngeal Carcinoma**, stage IV, now under *symptom‑directed* (palliation‑focused) management. Disease burden primarily localized to nasopharynx with minor distant pulmonary deposits that appear biologically indolent. Prior therapies exhausted; patient declines further cytotoxic regimens. **Intermittent Epistaxis** – Likely driven by fragile neoplastic vasculature and prior embolization sequelae. Current volume losses contributing to progressive anemia. **Anemia, Chronic** – Hb 9.6 g/dL, iron studies pending; presumed multifactorial (blood loss, inflammatory suppression). **Provoked Deep Vein Thrombosis – Left Femoral**, adequately anticoagulated with therapeutic enoxaparin; no evidence of propagation. **Hypertension, Controlled** – Previously exacerbated during cisplatin infusions; presently stable on lisinopril 20 mg daily. **Functional Status** – ECOG 2 (ambulatory, capable of self‑care but unable to carry out work activities). Overall prognosis remains guarded; median overall survival for heavily pretreated recurrent NPC approximates 12–18 months, though individual course varies widely

2021-02-02 pathology_report **Pathology Report** --- **Patient:** Gerald Thompson **Age/Sex:** 80 y / Male **Medical Record #:** [REDACTED] **Specimen Identification:** Bronchoscopic forceps biopsies – Right lower lobe segmental bronchus (B10) **Date of Procedure:** ________________________ **Type of Specimen:** Tissue core biopsies obtained via flexible bronchoscopy; formalin‐fixed, paraffin‑embedded (FFPE). --- ### Microscopic Description Histologic examination reveals infiltrative neoplastic proliferation composed predominantly of **moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma** displaying mixed **lepidic** and **acinar** growth patterns: * **Lepidic component:** Tumor cells spread along alveolar septa preserving underlying architecture, forming linear sheets of cuboidal-to-columnar epithelium with mildly enlarged nuclei, fine chromatin, inconspicuous nucleoli, and scant cytoplasm. Background lung parenchyma shows emphysematous changes without significant inflammation. --- ### Immunohistochemical Profile (Ancillary Studies) | Marker | Result | |--------|--------| | **TTF‑1** (Thyroid Transcription Factor‑1) | Positive (nuclear staining in ≥90% of tumor cells) | | **Napsin A** | Positive (cytoplasmic staining in majority of tumor cells) | | **p40/p63** | Negative (rules out squamous differentiation) | | **CK7** | Positive | | **CK20** | Negative | These markers confirm pulmonary origin and favor adenocarcinoma histotype. --- ### Molecular & Microsatellite Testing * **Mismatch Repair Proteins (MMR):** MLH1, PMS2, MSH2, and MSH6 show retained nuclear expression in both tumor and internal controls → *Intact*. * **Microsatellite Instability (MSI) PCR panel:** Stable across five mononucleotide repeats → *Microsatellite Stable (MSS)*. **Primary Lung Adenocarcinoma**, moderately differentiated, with combined lepidic and acinar architectural patterns. - Immunophenotype: TTF‑1⁺, Napsin A⁺, CK7⁺, p40⁻, CK20⁻. - Mismatch repair proficiency (MLH1/PMS2/MSH2/MSH6 intact); microsatellite stable
2021-05-14 ngs_report **Next‑Generation Sequencing (NGS) Report – Comprehensive Genomic Profiling** --- ### Patient Information - **Name:** Gerald Thompson - **Age/Sex:** 80‑year‑old Male ### Specimen Details | Item | Description | |--------------------------|-------------| | **Specimen ID** | BRONCHO‑BIOP‑2023‑01 | | **Source** | Bronchoscopic core needle biopsy of right lower lobe lesion | | **Date of Collection** | *[date withheld]* | | **Type of Specimen** | Formalin‑fixed paraffin‑embedded (FFPE) tissue block, ≥ 10 µm scrolls | | **Tumor Content Estimate** | Approx. ### Detected Somatic Alterations | Gene | Alteration | Protein Change | Allele Frequency / Copy Number | Interpretation | |-----------|----------------------------------------|----------------------------|---------------------------------|---------------| | **KRAS** | c.35G>T | p.Gly12Cys (p.G12C) | 38 % | Activating missense mutation typical of smoking‑related adenocarcinoma; predicts sensitivity to selective KRAS^G12C inhibitors (e.g., sotorasib, adagrasib). ### Interpretative Commentary Gerald Thompson’s metastatic non‑small cell lung carcinoma harbors several co‑occurring driver and tumor suppressor abnormalities characteristic of aggressive, smoker‑associated biology. The KRAS p.G12C mutation represents a potentially actionable target pending availability of approved KRAS^G12C inhibitors. Concurrent STK11 truncation and KEAP1 missense changes are recognized modifiers of both prognostic outlook and likelihood of benefit from anti‑PD‑(L)1 strategies, aligning with his documented low PD‑L1 expression (TPS 5%). MET copy‑number elevation is modest and currently below most clinical actionability thresholds. These molecular insights complement existing histopathologic data (moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, TTF‑1/Napsin A positive, MSS) and provide a foundation for individualized therapeutic decision‑making within the constraints of the patient’s overall health status and ongoing clinical considerations
2021-09-08 clinical_note ## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS Gerald Thompson is an 80‑year‑old gentleman who presented three weeks ago following incidental discovery of a right hilar pulmonary nodule on routine chest radiograph performed for evaluation of chronic cough. Subsequent work‑up—including bronchoscopy with biopsies, molecular profiling, and staging investigations—has established: | Item | Details | |------|---------| | Primary tumor | Right upper lobe adenocarcinoma, cT2 (≈4 cm) | | Nodal disease | Bilateral mediastinal & supraclavicular nodes, cN3 | | Distant metastases | Brain (multiple contrast‑enhancing lesions), bone (vertebral bodies L2–S1, ribs), bilateral adrenal glands (≈2 cm each) | | Histopathology | Moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with lepidic + acinar growth; TTF‑1⁺, Napsin A⁺; Ki‑67 ≈35% | | Molecular profile (NGS) | KRAS p.G12C VAF 38%; STK11 p.Q170* VAF 34%; TP53 p.R175H VAF 45%; KEAP1 p.R320C VAF 28%; MET copy‑gain 4.1×; EGFR WT; MSS; PD‑L1 TPS 5% | | Performance status | ECOG 3 (requires assistance for most activities of daily living; limited ambulation secondary to bony pain) | | Recent therapies | Enrolled six months ago in a phase I trial of a 4‑1BB recombinant fusion protein (agonistic costimulatory antibody). Developed Grade 3 cytokine‑release syndrome necessitating ICU admission, high‑dose methylprednisolone (1 mg/kg q24h × 3 days) followed by oral prednisone taper over four weeks. No residual organ dysfunction documented beyond mild transient transaminase rise. Current medication regimen consists solely of home antihypertensive agents (lisinopril 10 mg daily) and a short course of prednisone completed two weeks ago. Given the extent of visceral disease, poor functional reserve, and previous serious immune‑related toxicity, a thorough discussion regarding prognosis, therapeutic intent, and quality‑of‑life considerations was undertaken today. - **Brain MRI w/ gadolinium:** Four enhancing intra‑axial lesions (largest 1.2 cm in left parietal lobe) with surrounding vasogenic edema; no midline shift. - **Whole‑body PET/CT (performed 10 days prior):** Hypermetabolic activity concordant with above pulmonary and nodal disease; intense uptake in vertebrae T10, L2, sacrum, and bilateral adrenal glands (SUVmax 7.8). No additional distant foci. - **Bone Scan:** Multiple areas of increased tracer uptake throughout axial skeleton compatible with osteoblastic metastases. Overall impression: Advanced-stage (cStage IV) KRAS‑mutated adenocarcinoma of the lung with multi‑organ dissemination (brain, bone, adrenal, pleura). Disease burden is substantial; physiologic reserve markedly compromised. Molecular landscape notable for concurrent STK11 truncating mutation, TP53 missense alteration, and KEAP1 variant—all associated with aggressive phenotype and diminished responsiveness to checkpoint inhibition. 2019-05-09 ngs_report **Next‑Generation Sequencing Report – Comprehensive Genomic Profiling** --- ### Patient Information - **Name:** Daniel Morales - **Medical Record #:** [REDACTED] - **Date of Birth / Age:** 30 y/o (sample obtained at age 28) - **Sex:** Male ### Specimen Details | Item | Description | |--------------------------|------------------------------------------------| | **Specimen ID** | SKN-META‑001 | | **Source** | Excisional biopsy of cutaneous metastasis (lower back) | | **Collection Date** | [date omitted – to be inserted electronically] | | **Preservation** | Formalin‑fixed paraffin‑embedded (FFPE) tissue block | | **Tumor Content** | Estimated >60 % neoplastic cells (H&E review) | ### Test Ordered - **Assay:** Whole‑Exome Next‑Generation Sequencing (NGS) – 800‑Gene Oncology Panel - **Platform:** Illumina NovaSeq 6000, paired‑end 150 bp reads - **Coverage Goal:** Minimum average depth 300× across target regions; ≥99 % bases covered ≥100× ### Methodology Summary DNA extracted from FFPE material underwent quality control (QC). **Core Colorectal Cancer Drivers** – The concurrent presence of an APC truncating mutation, TP53 hotspot missense, and biallelic loss of SMAD4 aligns with canonical pathways implicated in sporadic, microsatellite-stable colorectal carcinoma. Their collective profile supports a biologically aggressive phenotype, concordant with the patient’s Stage IV presentation featuring cutaneous dissemination. **Cell Cycle Dysregulation** – Amplification of CDK4 suggests heightened cyclin D–CDK4/6 activity, potentially rendering the tumor susceptible to CDK4/6 inhibition. While such agents are approved primarily for breast malignancies, investigational protocols exist for solid tumors harboring CDK4 amplifications. **HER2 Subclonality** – An ERBB2 S310F alteration is detected at low allelic abundance, indicative of a minor subclone. 2020-02-14 pathology_report **PATHOLOGY REPORT** --- **Patient:** Priya Desai **MRN:** [Redacted] **Specimen ID:** MED‐001 (Mediastinal Lymph Node Excised Biopsy) **Date of Collection / Receipt:** _______________________ ### SPECIMEN TYPE & CLINICAL INFORMATION - **Procedure:** Excisional biopsy of a right paratracheal mediastinal lymph node performed via video‑assisted thoracoscopy. - **Clinical History:** 30‑year‑old woman with newly diagnosed Stage IV nodal marginal zone lymphoma (B‑cell NHL); presenting with fever, night sweats, unintentional weight loss, and bulky adenopathy involving cervical, mediastinal, and inguinal stations. ### GROSS DESCRIPTION Received in formalin is a single oval lymphoid fragment measuring **2.7 × 1.9 × 1.2 cm**, weighing **3.4 g**. There is no evidence of sheets of centroblasts, mantle cell proliferation, or high‑grade transformation. Mitotic activity is low; Ki‑67 proliferative index approximates **~25 %** in hotspot areas. ### IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY (IHC) | Marker | Result | |--------|--------| | **CD20** | Positive (strong membranous) | | **CD79a** | Positive | | **BCL2** | Positive | | **Ki‑67** | Approximately 25 % (hotspots) | | **CD5** | Negative | | **Cyclin D1** | Negative | | **CD10** | Negative | | **BCL6** | Negative | | **MUM1/IRF4** | Weak focal positivity | | **IgM** | Polytypic expression | | **Kappa/Lambda light chains** | Polyclonal distribution | Ancillary flow cytometry corroborated a monotypic B‑cell population expressing CD19, CD20, CD22, FMC7, lacking CD5 and CD23. Light chain restriction was not demonstrable, supporting a polyclonal/plasmacytic component rather than overt clonality beyond morphology. These findings correlate with the known clinical presentation of advanced-stage nodal marginal zone lymphoma
2020-05-12 ngs_report **Molecular Oncology – Next Generation Sequencing Report** --- ### Patient Information **Name:** Priya Desai **Age/Sex:** 30/Female ### Specimen Details | Item | Description | |--------------------------|-------------| | **Specimen ID** | MEDIASTINAL_NODE_001 | | **Source** | Excisional biopsy of mediastinal lymph node (formalin‐fixed paraffin‑embedded block) | | **Date of Collection** | *[date omitted]* | | **Requested Test(s)** | Comprehensive solid‑tumor panel (DNA‑based) including single‑nucleotide variants, small insertions/deletions, selected copy‑number alterations, and calculation of Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB) | ### Testing Platform / Methodology - DNA extracted using QIAamp FFPE Tissue Kit (Qiagen); quantity ≥200 ng, quality A260/A280 ≈1.85. - **Chromosome 18:** Gain (trisomy) – inferred from normalized read depth (+1 copy). #### Other Biomarkers - **Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB):** 5.0 muts/Mb (Low). - **Microsatellite Instability (MSI):** Stable (MSI‑S). ### Interpretative Summary The comprehensive NGS assay identified several genetic abnormalities characteristic of nodal marginal‑zone B‑cell lymphoma: 1. **NOTCH2 truncating mutation** and **KMT2D nonsense mutation** represent established drivers in marginal‑zone lymphoproliferations, supporting the morphologic diagnosis obtained from the excisional biopsy. Their relatively high VAFs suggest they reside in the dominant malignant clone. The presence of **trisomy 3 and trisomy 18** aligns with known cytogenetic hallmarks of marginal‑zone lymphoma and contributes additional evidence of clonality. An **EZH2 p.Tyr641Asn** hotspot alteration is detected at low allelic fraction. While EZH2 gain‑of‑function mutations are more prevalent in follicular and diffuse large B‑cell lymphomas, occasional occurrence in marginal‑zone cases has been reported. Its minor representation could reflect either a subclone or technical artifact; therefore, its clinical relevance remains uncertain. Additional low‑frequency variants in **CREBBP**, **ARID1A**, and **DNMT3A** are most plausibly passenger events without clear pathogenetic implication for this disease entity. Overall, the molecular landscape corroborates the pathological finding of marginal‑zone lymphoma and does not reveal actionable targets currently approved for routine therapeutic decision making in this setting. Emerging agents targeting NOTCH pathway dysregulation or EZH2 inhibition remain investigational and were not administered to the patient at the time of this test

On prednisone 40 mg → tapered to 15 mg, hydroxychloroquine, MMF. **Hypertension – suboptimally controlled** – Despite ACE inhibitor and thiazide diuretic; BP averages ≈150/90 mmHg. **Performance Status** – ECOG 1–2, ambulatory, independent ADLs. 2016-11-06 clinical_note --- ### HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS Michael Rivera is a 58‑year‑old man with recently treated p16‑positive, stage III (T3 N1 M0) oropharyngeal SCC of the base of tongue who presents for routine follow‑up approximately nine months after completing definitive concurrent chemoradiotherapy (weekly cisplatin + IMRT 70 Gy). He tolerated the acute toxicities of treatment reasonably well aside from expected Grade 3 oral mucositis (requiring NG feeding during weeks 3–4) and transient nausea/vomiting (Grade 2). Post‑therapy recovery was uncomplicated apart from a short course of speech therapy for Grade 2 dysphagia at 2 weeks post‑radiation. At three months post‑completion, FDG‑PET/CT demonstrated essentially complete metabolic response with only a tiny residual subcentimeter left‑level II node (SUV 2.1) judged indeterminate. Since the last clinic encounter four weeks ago, the patient notes a gradual onset of mild pressure and occasional tenderness localized to the right lower neck (right Level III distribution). Given the concerning symptomatology, a contrast‑enhanced MRI of the neck obtained today (see “Imaging Results”) demonstrates a newly visualized 1.2 cm enhancing soft‑tissue focus within a right Level III cervical node, raising suspicion for locoregional recurrence. No additional abnormal lymphadenopathy elsewhere in the neck. The remainder of the head & neck anatomy shows postoperative scarring consistent with prior radiotherapy but no overt masses. Overall, the picture is most compatible with early nodal recurrence despite otherwise favorable interim assessments. Right neck: mobile, mildly tender 1‑2 cm firm node deep to sternocleidomastoid at Level III; overlying skin intact, no erythema. *Skin:* No active dermatitis; faint residual papulopustular lesions on cheeks (grade 1 acneiform rash). Node exhibits restricted diffusion (low ADC) suggestive of viable neoplasm. No capsular breach, extracapsular spread, nor invasion of surrounding musculature, vessels, or airway structures observed. - **Other Structures:** Oropharynx demonstrates post‑therapeutic fibrosis; no residual primary mass. **Impression:** New isolated right Level III cervical lymph node highly suspicious for locoregional recurrence of HPV‑associated oropharyngeal SCC. No evidence of additional regional or distant disease on current study. **Recurrent/persistent disease** – Isolated right Level III nodal recurrence, confirmed radiographically (MRI) with correlating mild symptomatic discomfort. Prior definitive CRT achieved excellent local control of primary site; this represents the first documented failure. **Status post concurrent chemoradiation** – Completed 8‑week IMRT (70 Gy) plus weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m² eight weeks ago; tolerability overall good with manageable acute toxicity. **Comorbidities** – Hypertension controlled on lisinopril 10 mg daily; otherwise healthy. **Medication adherence** – Currently off systemic anticancer agents; taking vitamin D supplement, antihypertensive, stool softener prn. **Transoral Robotic Surgery (TORS)/Selective Neck Dissection** – Preferred for isolated nodal disease with limited volume; anticipate R0 resection with preservation of function. **Re‑irradiation** – Considered if surgical morbidity unacceptable or patient declines operation; requires careful dosimetric planning (cumulative dose constraints to spinal cord, brainstem, mandible). May combine with radiosensitizer (cetuximab) versus altered fractionation schedule. **Systemic Therapy Options** – In setting of unresectable/refractory disease, explore checkpoint inhibition (nivolumab or pembrolizumab) especially given HPV‑driven biology and modest PD‑L1 expression historically seen in such tumors. Enrollment in a phase II trial evaluating combined PD‑1 blockade with PI3Kα inhibitor (given emerging PIK3CA alteration data) could be discussed once genomic profile available

2017-04-23 ngs_report **Next‑Generation Sequencing (NGS) Report – Comprehensive Genomic Profiling** --- ### Patient Identification - **Name:** Michael Rivera - **Sex/Age:** Male, 58 years (at time of original diagnosis) ### Specimen Details | Item | Description | |------|-------------| | **Specimen Type** | Formalin‑Fixed Paraffin‑Embedded (FFPE) tissue block – salvage transoral robotic resection (right Level III cervical nodal metastasis) | | **Anatomic Site** | Base of Tongue / Cervical Lymph Node (Level III) | | **Collection Date** | Month 10 (post‑salvage surgery) | | **Tumor Content Estimate** | Approx. 0.02 copies/cell) | Viral integration confirmed | — | Consistent with p16⁺ phenotype | \*Allele frequencies represent proportion of mutant alleles among total sequenced reads at the locus (adjusted for estimated tumor purity). #### Additional Biomarkers - **Tumor Mutation Burden (TMB):** 6 mut/Mb (low) – calculated over the coding region covered by the assay. - **Microsatellite Stability (MSI):** Stable (MSI‑S). **PIK3CA p.H1047R** – A canonical activating hotspot found in ∼15 % of head‑and‑neck SCCs, particularly HPV‑associated tumors. This alteration predicts sensitivity to PI3Kα inhibitors (e.g., alpelisib) under investigation in combination regimens; however, FDA‑approved agents for this indication remain limited outside breast cancer. **CDKN2A Homozygous Deletion** – Loss of both alleles leads to deregulation of the RB pathway and has been associated with aggressive behavior in oropharyngeal SCC. May confer susceptibility to CDK4/6 inhibition, though clinical benefit in this setting is still exploratory. **EGFR Copy Number Gain** – Approximately three‑fold increase suggests upregulation of EGFR signaling. Historically linked to responsiveness to anti‑EGFR monoclonal antibodies such as cetuximab, which aligns with the current adjuvant regimen administered to the patient. **NOTCH1 p.L1575P** – Classified as a VUS due to insufficient functional data. Notch pathway perturbations occur variably in head‑and‑neck malignancies; ongoing trials are evaluating gamma‑secretase inhibitors. **HPV‑16 Integration** – Confirms etiologic role of high‑risk human papillomavirus, supporting the observed strong p16 IHC expression. HPV‑positive oropharyngeal cancers typically demonstrate favorable prognosis compared with HPV‑negative counterparts. **Low TMB and MSI‑Stable Status** – Suggest limited likelihood of robust responses to checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy solely based on neoantigen load, although other factors (viral antigens) may modulate immune responsiveness. EGFR amplification supports continued utilization of cetuximab, as currently prescribed. - **Prognostic Insight:** Presence of CDKN2A loss and EGFR amplification may portend higher risk of locoregional recurrence despite clear surgical margins; close radiographic surveillance recommended. - **Therapeutic Resistance Markers:** No emergent resistance-associated EGFR kinase domain mutations were identified (e.g., exon 20 insertions), nor were there secondary TP53 alterations

(c.6115G>A) | Missense change in intracellular ankyrin repeats | 18 % | | **CREBBP** | p.Tyr1460His (c.4379T>C) | Missense alteration in HAT domain | 24 % | | **PTEN** | Loss (absence of detectable *ROS1* fusions, or other actionable kinase drivers commonly associated with non–small cell lung carcinoma. ### Interpretation The genomic profile demonstrates hallmark alterations characteristic of de‑differentiated neuroendocrine malignancies such as extensive‑stage small‑cell lung carcinoma (SCLC): * Co‑occurring **TP53** missense mutation and **RB1** truncating loss represent near‑universal early events driving uncontrolled proliferation and defective G₁ checkpoint regulation in SCLC. Their presence confirms the expected lineage biology of the current tumor. * **MYC** amplification (high copy number) further supports aggressive phenotypic behavior and has been linked to rapid growth kinetics and resistance to conventional chemotherapy in SCLC cohorts. * Additional aberrations—including **PTEN** homozygous loss, **CCNE1** amplification, and **NOTCH3**/*CREBBP* missense changes—suggest secondary pathways contributing to dysregulated PI3K/AKT signaling, cell cycle acceleration, and epigenetic remodeling. While these are recurrent in various solid tumors, their isolated predictive value in SCLC remains investigational. * The low‑frequency **MET exon 14 skipping** variant is atypical for pure SCLC but has been sporadically reported in mixed neuroendocrine carcinomas and could reflect intratumoral heterogeneity or a minor clone harboring a potentially targetable alteration. Its modest allelic fraction indicates limited clonal dominance at the time of sampling. Overall, the mutational landscape aligns with classical SCLC genomics, underscoring the biologic aggressiveness observed clinically. The identification of a MET exon 14 skipping event, albeit at low level, provides a rationale for consideration of enrollment in trials investigating MET inhibitors, pending confirmation of functional expression
2013-06-23 clinical_note ### HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS Thomas Lee was diagnosed with extensive‑stage small‑cell lung carcinoma (AJCC 8th ed., T4 N3 M1c, Stage IV) involving the right main bronchus, mediastinum, contralateral lungs, left adrenal gland, and osseous sites (sternum, left humerus). He presented initially with severe cough, daily hemoptysis (~40 mL), and ECOG PS = 2. Initial staging PET/CT confirmed widespread metabolically active disease. Molecular testing performed on an EBUS‑TBNA sample of a subcarinal lymph node returned the following somatic alterations: | Gene | Variant | Allelic Fraction / Copy # | |------|---------|----------------------------| | **TP53** | p.R273C | VAF 52% | | **RB1** | Frameshift del c.1335delA | — | | **MYC** | Amplification | CN ~15 | | **NOTCH3** | p.E2039K | — | | **CREBBP** | p.Y1460H | — | | **PTEN** | Loss (protein expression) | — | | **CCNE1** | Amplification | — | | **MET** | Exon 14 skipping (low‑level) | — | No activating alterations were detected in EGFR, KRAS, ALK, ROS1, or other canonical targetable drivers. Lee has undergone palliative EBRT to a painful left humeral lesion (20 Gy/5 fx) with symptomatic improvement, and supportive care for intermittent hemoptysis (humidified O₂, tranexamic acid). Recent restaging CT demonstrates modest progression of the primary mass (from 6.5 cm → 6.8 cm) and development of a right pleural effusion; thoracentesis confirms malignant involvement. His functional status has deteriorated to ECOG = 3 secondary to dyspnea and fatigue. - Musculoskeletal: Bone pain localized to left humerus (improved post‑radiation) and lower back (new L2/L4 lesions pending palliation). ### IMAGING SUMMARY - **Contrast‑Enhanced CT Chest (repeat)** – Slight increase in size of right main bronchus mass (now 6.8 cm), persistent encasement of right PA, interval development of right pleural fluid collection measuring up to 1.5 cm posteriorly. Mediastinal nodal stations remain bulky. Bilateral peripheral pulmonary nodules stable. - **MRI Brain w/o Contrast** – No enhancing parenchymal lesions; ventricles unremarkable. - **FDG‑PET/CT (prior)** – Hypermetabolism in primary, mediastinum, left adrenal, sternal and humeral lesions; baseline for comparison. **Core Molecular Drivers** *Co‑loss of TP53 and RB1* remains the hallmark genetic signature of de‑differentiated neuroendocrine tumors such as SCLC. Both alterations are clonal (high VAF) and support the aggressive phenotype observed clinically. ***MYC* Amplification** High‑level copy gain (CN ≈ 15) aligns with rapid proliferative capacity and resistance mechanisms seen in many relapsed SCLC cohorts. Literature correlates MYC amplification with poorer response rates to conventional etoposide/platinum regimens and suggests heightened sensitivity to agents disrupting transcriptional addiction (e.g., CDK7 inhibitors) – however, none are currently FDA‑approved outside investigational protocols. ***MET* Exon 14 Skipping Variant** Detected at low allele frequency, this splice alteration is atypical for pure SCLC but has been sporadically reported (<5%) in combined small‑cell/non‑small‑cell phenotypes and “transformed” NSCLC. Functional consequence includes ligand‑independent activation of MET signaling pathways, potentially conferring a growth advantage and offering a theoretical therapeutic niche for MET tyrosine kinase inhibition. Given the paucity of robust efficacy data in isolated SCLC, the finding is catalogued primarily for trial eligibility screening rather than immediate off‑label prescribing. **Additional Alterations** - *PTEN loss*: May augment PI3K/AKT pathway activity, contributing to chemoresistance. - *CCNE1 amplification*: Suggestive of cyclin‑E driven G1–S transition acceleration; experimental CDK2 blockade under investigation. - *NOTCH3* missense change and *CREBBP* mutation are regarded as passenger events in most SCLC series. Overall, the composite genotype underscores a highly proliferative, genomically unstable tumor lacking actionable targets approved for routine practice, but highlighting several avenues for enrollment into early‑phase studies (e.g., MET inhibitors, BET bromodomain antagonists, Aurora kinase inhibitors). Extensive‑stage small‑cell lung carcinoma, progressive despite best supportive measures; molecular profile reveals classical SCLC drivers (TP53/RB1 loss, MYC amplification) plus a low‑frequency MET exon 14 skipping alteration of uncertain significance in this context. Symptomatic right pleural effusion causing dyspnea—requiring ongoing drainage considerations. Mild hypercalcemia secondary to bony metastatic disease. Declining performance status (ECOG 3) limiting tolerance for intensive chemotherapy

2014-06-02 ngs_report ### Detected Genomic Alterations | Gene / Region | Variant Type | Specific Change | Allele Frequency* | Interpretation | |------------------------------|-------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|-------------------|----------------| | **IGH‑MMSET (FGFR3)** | Translocation (recurrent) | t(4;14)(p16.3;q32.3) → IGH::MMSET fusion | — | High‑risk cytogenetic abnormality characteristic of aggressive MM; previously reported in this patient. ### Additional Molecular Findings - **Microsatellite Instability (MSI):** Stable (no evidence of MSI‑high phenotype). - **Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB):** Low (~1.7 mut/Mb). No pathogenic variants were identified in the following clinically relevant loci: **NRAS**, **BRAF**, **PIK3CA**, **CDKN2A/B**, **RB1**, **CCND1**, **DIS3**, **FAM46C**, **IRF4**, **MAX**, **MAPK1**, **STAT3**, **TRAPPC9**, **ZFHX4**. The acetabular plasmacytoma harbors a **high‑risk genomic signature** characterized by: - Persistent **IGH‑MMSET (t(4;14))** translocation with accompanying **FGFR3 amplification**—both hallmark features of aggressive multiple myeloma and predictors of inferior overall survival. - A **novel high‑level MYC amplification** (copy number ≈12), absent in earlier specimens, indicating clonal evolution toward increased proliferation and potential resistance to standard proteasome‑IMiD regimens. - Co‑existing **TP53 loss‑of‑function** mutation (R248W) and **KRAS activating** mutation, both contributing to chemotherapy refractoriness. - Continued **1q21 gain**, reinforcing the high‑risk category. The combination of **t(4;14)**, **del(13q14)** (historically known), **TP53 mutation**, and now **MYC amplification** places this disease in the highest tier of prognostic models used for relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma (e.g., Revised International Staging System [R‑ISS] Stage III equivalent). While **FGFR3 amplification** persists and may render the clone susceptible to FGFR‑targeted agents, the emergence of **MYC amplification** suggests additional pathways driving growth independent of FGFR signaling. No actionable alterations amenable to FDA‑approved therapies outside of ongoing investigational protocols were identified (e.g., lack of BCL‑2 dependence markers such as **t(14;16)** or **t(14;20)**, absence of **SLFN11** expression data). ### Technical Notes & Limitations - The assay does not reliably detect balanced chromosomal translocations lacking breakpoints within captured intronic regions; however, the presence of the canonical **IGH‑MMSET** fusion was inferred from the recurrent breakpoint pattern and corroborated by previous FISH testing. ### Conclusion This comprehensive profiling confirms persistence of the original high‑risk **IGH‑MMSET/FGRF3** genotype together with acquisition of a **de novo MYC amplification**, underscoring biologic aggressiveness and likely contribution to recent clinical progression despite prior lines of therapy. The molecular landscape supports consideration of strategies targeting FGFR3 (if available) alongside agents capable of overcoming MYC‑driven proliferation, acknowledging the limited efficacy of conventional IMiDs and proteasome inhibitors observed in this heavily pretreated setting
